perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and perfor mance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of _____________________ (state) applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof . The Federal and state courts located in _______________ (state) shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under , or in any way connected with or related to, the terms of this Agreement and Receiving Party: (i) consents to pe rsonal jurisdiction therein; and (ii) waives the right to raise forum non conveniens or any similar objection.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of _____________________ (state) applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof . The Federal and state courts located in _______________ (state) shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under , or in any way connected with or related to, the terms of this Agreement and Receiving Party consents to personal jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the waiver of 'forum non conveniens', the Receiving Party retains the right to argue that the chosen forum is inconvenient, potentially conflicting with laws designed to ensure accessible legal recourse.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) allows for the transfer of a case to a more convenient forum. By removing the waiver of 'forum non conveniens', the Receiving Party could invoke this statute. While the original clause restricted this right via contractual agreement, the modification gives the Receiving Party an opportunity to exploit forum shopping, possibly contradicting the principles of fair and efficient legal proceedings as interpreted within the broader legal system and the spirit of the statute that allows transfer for convenience.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The removal of the waiver of 'forum non conveniens' in the changed text directly contradicts the original agreement by allowing the Receiving Party to argue that the chosen forum is inconvenient. This contradicts the original intent to ensure disputes are resolved in a specific forum, as initially agreed. The law snippet from 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a) grants the district court the power to transfer any civil action to any other district or division for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and the removal of the waiver now permits the Receiving Party to invoke this statute. While parties can contractually agree to waive this right, the modification enables forum shopping, potentially undermining fair legal proceedings.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall immediately return and redeliver to Disclosing Party all tangible mater ial embodying any Confidential Information provided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and all other documents or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of t he foregoing, including “copies” that have been converted to computerized media in the form of image, data , word processing , or other types of files either manually or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form o f storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request ; provided however that the Receiving Party may retain such of its documents as is necessary to enable it to comply with its reasonable document retention policies .
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall immediately return and redeliver to Disclosing Party all tangible material embodying any Confidential Information provided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and all other documents or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of the foregoing, including “copies” that have been converted to computerized media in the form of image, data, word processing, or other types of files either manually or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form of storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the Receiving Party to retain documents necessary to comply with reasonable document retention policies. By omitting this, the modified contract forces the Receiving Party to return all documents regardless of legal or regulatory obligations to retain them, potentially violating document retention laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 outlines the requirements for brokers and dealers to preserve certain records. By forcing the Receiving Party to return all confidential information without regard to document retention policies, the modified agreement could compel a regulated entity to violate SEC regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the clause allowing the Receiving Party to retain documents necessary for compliance with document retention policies, which directly contradicts 17 CFR § 240.17a-4, as explained by the LLM. The scraped snippet 1 confirms the existence of this regulation, titled 'Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers.' The original contract allowed retention for compliance, but the changed contract forces the return of all documents regardless of regulatory needs, creating a clear legal contradiction by potentially compelling a regulated entity to violate SEC regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, both parties hereby agree that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief preventing the dissemination of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof. Such injunctive relief shall be in addi tion to any other remedies available hereunder, whether at law or in equity. Disclosing Party shall be entitled to recover its costs and fees, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, incurred in obtaining any such relief. Further, in the event of litigatio n relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorney’s fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, both parties hereby agree that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to injunctive relief preventing the dissemination of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof. Such injunctive relief shall be in addi tion to any other remedies available hereunder, whether at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that allows the prevailing party to recover reasonable attorney's fees, the contract discourages parties from fully pursuing their legal rights, potentially violating principles ensuring equal access to justice and fair compensation for legal harm, particularly if the removed clause would violate applicable state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally allows parties to agree on who pays attorney's fees. However, removing the clause that assigns attorney's fees to the prevailing party may contradict specific California laws or judicial precedents if the nature of the case or a related statute already mandates fee-shifting in certain circumstances. This omission could be problematic if another law or circumstance would mandate attorneys fees to the prevailing party. By removing this clause, this would prevent a party that has been harmed from being made whole.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes a clause that allows the prevailing party to recover reasonable attorney's fees. The Law Explanation suggests that removing this clause may contradict California laws if a related statute already mandates fee-shifting. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1021, states that attorney's fees are generally left to the agreement of the parties, unless specifically provided for by statute. The Perturbation Explanation suggests the change discourages parties from pursuing their legal rights fully, potentially violating principles ensuring equal access to justice. While CCP 1021 allows parties to agree on attorney's fees, the potential for other statutes to mandate fee-shifting means removing the clause could create a legal contradiction if such a statute applies, because it could prevent a harmed party from being made whole, as the LLM explanation states. This fits the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction, because the removal of the clause creates a legal loophole.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices or communications required or permitted to be given hereunde r may be delivered by hand, deposited with a nationally recognized overnight carrier, electronic -mail, or mailed by certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, in each case, to the address of the other party first indicated above (or such ot her addressee as may be furnished by a party in accordance with this paragraph). All such notices or communications shall be deemed to have been given and received (a) in the case of personal delivery or electronic -mail, on the date of such delivery, (b) in the case of delivery by a nationally recognized overnight carrier, on the third business day following dispatch and (c) in the case of mailing, on the seventh business day following such mailing.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices or communications required or permitted to be given hereunder may be delivered by hand or mailed by certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, in each case, to the address of the other party first indicated above (or such other addressee as may be furnished by a party in accordance with this paragraph). All such notices or communications shall be deemed to have been given and received (a) in the case of personal delivery, on the date of such delivery and (b) in the case of mailing, on the seventh business day following such mailing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'electronic-mail' and 'nationally recognized overnight carrier' as acceptable forms of communication and their corresponding receipt times, the contract limits the methods for providing notice. This conflicts with the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act), which gives electronic signatures and records the same legal effect as traditional signatures, provided certain conditions are met.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 7001 - General rule of validity
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 7001 states that electronic signatures and records shall not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because they are in electronic form. Removing electronic mail as a means of delivering notice could potentially conflict with this law by placing an undue burden on parties to deliver notice by traditional means. This is especially important when coupled with removal of nationally recognized overnight carrier, as it could be argued that the contract is designed to make it harder to provide notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 7001 - General rule of validity | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 7001 - General rule of validity U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) In general Notwithstanding any statute, regulation, or other rule of law (other than this subchapter and subchapter II), with respect to any transaction in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce— (1) a signature, contract, or other record relating to such transaction may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because it is in electronic form; and (2) a contract relating to such transaction may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because an electronic signature or electronic record was used in its formation. (b) Preservation of rights and obligations This subchapter does not— (1) limit, alter, or otherwise affect any requirement imposed by a statute, regulation, or rule of law relating to the rights and obligations of persons under such statute, regulation, or rule of law other than a requirement that contracts or other records be written, signed, or in nonelectronic form; or (2) require any person to agree to use or accept electronic records or electronic signatures , other than a governmental agency with respect to a record other than a contract to which it is a party. (c) Consumer disclosures (1) Consent to electronic records Notwithstanding subsection (a), if a statute, regulation, or other rule of law requires that information relating to a transaction or transactions in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce be provided or made available to a consumer in writing, the use of an electronic record to provide or make available (whichever is required) such information satisfies the requirement that such information be in writing if— (A) the consumer has affirmatively consented to such use and has not withdrawn such consent; (B) the consu
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'electronic-mail' and 'nationally recognized overnight carrier' as acceptable forms of communication, which contradicts 15 U.S.C. § 7001. The scraped snippet from 15 U.S. Code § 7001 states that a contract may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because it is in electronic form. By removing electronic mail as a means of delivering notice, the modified contract is contradicting this law, as it is putting additional burden to the parties involved, limiting the ways to send the notice. This makes the contradiction explicit and impactful.
